problem_summary: 2018 MCM Problem C (data): four US states (AZ, CA, NM, TX) plan a joint clean-energy compact. Using the provided 50-year SEDS energy dataset, teams had to (1) characterize and compare each state's energy profile and use of cleaner/renewable energy, (2) decide which state had the "best" 2009 profile, (3) predict 2025 and 2050 profiles under no policy change, then (4) set realistic renewable-energy compact goals and recommend actions, packaged with a governor's memo. This O-award paper (Team 82150) frames the whole thing around a "3E principle" (energy-economy-environment) and delivers a multi-stage pipeline: profile analysis -> multi-criteria ranking -> VAR forecasting -> game-theoretic/genetic optimization of the compact -> three concrete policy levers.
models_used: Data triage taxonomy: classify SEDS 'msn' records into redundant / invalid / normal, then dedup (e.g. NGACB vs NNACB) and drop empty codes (PACCK) — implemented in a MATLAB getMsn.m lookup script

Four custom 3E indices defined from raw data: A1 Renewable Energy Potential = (RP_t - RP_{t-1})/RP_{t-1} (YoY growth of renewable production); A2 Energy Structure = 1 - (1/4)Σ|EP_i - 1/4| (Hamming-style closeness of the 4-source electricity mix to a uniform 1/4-1/4-1/4-1/4 'standard' structure); A3 Renewable Proportion = RC_t/TC_t; A4 Economic Conversion Rate = GDP_t/RE_t

EM-TOPSIS-PROMETHEE composite evaluation model: Entropy Method for objective index weights W=[0.2209,0.3256,0.2136,0.2399]; TOPSIS for Euclidean distance to positive/negative ideal solutions; PROMETHEE outranking with a step preference function P(d)=1 if d>=0 and positive/negative outranking flows; final blended score S_i+ = ζ·Φ_i+ + τ·D_i-, ζ=τ=0.5. Result: CA 1.00, TX 0.46, AZ 0.40, NM 0.23

Panel-data regression with time-fixed effects for renewable-share drivers: y_it = β1·ln(pop) + β2·ln(GDP growth) + β3·ln(Grid Modernization Index) + β4·α_i(ocean dummy) + ε; found population (+0.023***) and Grid Modernization Index (+0.026***) significant, GDP growth and coastal geography not

Vector Autoregression (VAR) forecasting, instantiated as two named sub-models — ECPM (Energy Consumption Prediction Model) and SDPM (Sustainability Development Prediction Model). Endogenous vector per state = [Total Energy Consumption, average energy price, GDP growth rate, population]; ADF stationarity test + differencing/standardization; lag order p chosen by AIC (p=1); back-test accuracy 96.8-97.6% (ECPM) and 90.9-94.5% (SDPM). Forecasts 2025 & 2050

Constrained cost-minimization compact-allocation model with a game-theory 'trust' term: minimize p_e^i·E^i + p_e^n·E^n + p_p·P_TC + η/E^i (the η/E^i term penalizes distrust/encourages cooperation) subject to total-consumption balance, a renewable-proportion floor α, a self-sufficiency floor β, and E^i>0

Genetic Algorithm to solve the above combinatorial allocation (custom MATLAB fga: proportional selection, crossover, bit mutation, inversion; eranum=200, popsize=100, pCross=0.8, pMutation=0.1) — yields optimal renewable imports for TX/NM and exports for AZ at 2025/2050

Export 'pulling coefficient' economics to translate optimization outputs into goal deltas: ΔGDP = γ·p^e·ΔE^e (γ=0.667 from trade value-added literature) giving ΔA4, and budget-elasticity statements (e.g. +1% energy budget in TX -> +9.9% A3; +1% export multiplier in AZ -> +3.4% A4)
key_techniques: Self-defined, formula-backed indices instead of using raw dataset columns — every one of A1-A4 is a named, cited, equation-defined quantity, which makes the later ranking and goals feel principled rather than arbitrary

Stacking three MCDM methods (Entropy + TOPSIS + PROMETHEE) so weights are objective (entropy) AND the score captures both distance-to-ideal (TOPSIS) and pairwise outranking (PROMETHEE) — explicitly justified as giving both an absolute ideal comparison and relative inter-state comparison
Hamming-distance closeness to a uniform 'standard structure' as a clean, single-number diversity/balance metric for the energy mix

Treating the compact as an optimization with an embedded behavioral/game-theory trust term (η/E^i) rather than pure cost minimization — links the math model to the soft 'interstate communication' policy lever
AIC-driven VAR with ADF stationarity checking and explicit train/test back-testing to report numeric accuracy %, which preempts the judge's 'how do you know your forecast is any good' question
Sensitivity-style policy elasticities (X% input -> Y% goal metric) that connect each of the three recommended actions directly back to A3/A4, closing the loop from model to actionable advice
Memo-first packaging: a governor-facing memo placed up front in plain language, satisfying the ICM 'communicate to a decision-maker' deliverable
why_it_won: From a judge's seat, this paper wins because it is a fully closed loop where every modeling choice is later cashed out into a decision: the four 3E indices defined in Part I are the exact same A1-A4 that get ranked by EM-TOPSIS-PROMETHEE, forecast by VAR, optimized by the GA compact model, and finally moved by the three policy actions. Nothing is orphaned. It also demonstrates methodological maturity rare at this level — objective entropy weighting (no hand-picked weights), a hybrid MCDM that the team can articulate WHY they combined, back-tested VAR accuracy numbers (96-97%), and real appendix code in MATLAB/R that makes the work reproducible and credible. It directly answers each sub-prompt in order, handles the 'best profile' question with a defensible quantitative ranking (CA wins, with a nuanced 'TX is worst-now-but-best-prospects' reading that shows judgment), and it nails the ICM communication requirement with a clean governor's memo. The differentiated goals (import-oriented states chase A3 via subsidies; export-oriented Arizona chases A4 via tax cuts) show the team understood the states are not interchangeable.
section_highlights: Structure is textbook-strong and tuned for graders. The Summary Sheet states the whole pipeline (EM-TOPSIS-PROMETHEE -> VAR -> Game Theory + GA -> three actions) in two paragraphs. A governor's memo is hoisted to the very front in plain English (profile / prediction-without-policy / recommended goals), satisfying the decision-maker deliverable before any math. A dedicated Data Processing section (redundant vs invalid vs normal 'msn' codes) signals respect for the 'data' problem type and earns trust. Part I marches profiles -> evolution -> best-profile ranking -> prediction; Part II marches optimal-allocation -> goals -> three concrete actions, mirroring the prompt's two halves. Heavy, consistent use of named acronyms (3E, ECPM, SDPM, EM-TOPSIS-PROMETHEE) gives the reader handles. An honest Strengths/Weaknesses section, a back-testing accuracy table (Table 4-4), explicit assumptions, full nomenclature table, 13 cited references, and an appendix with runnable MATLAB/R code round it out. Figures (choropleth Grid Modernization map, stacked sector bars, GA fitness convergence, VAR forecast curves) carry the comparative story visually.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Massive simplifying assumption that 'renewable energy = electricity' and that total consumption = renewable + petroleum only — the team flags it, but it distorts every renewable metric (geothermal/wind heat, biomass, hydro outside electricity are dropped)

The A2 'standard structure' = uniform 1/4 across Geothermal/Nuclear/Photovoltaic/Wind is arbitrary; the team admits each state's ideal mix should differ by resource endowment. It also counts Nuclear as 'renewable', which is contestable

Tiny cross-section: only 4 states means the entropy weights, TOPSIS ideal solutions and especially the panel regression (n≈4 states) rest on very few units — significance stars on the regression are statistically fragile

VAR is fit per-state on a short annual series and forecast 40 years to 2050; reported 96-97% accuracy is in-sample-style back-test on a handful of held-out years and almost certainly overstates true 2050 reliability. Long-horizon VAR compounds error fast

Several embedded figures/numbers in the markdown look noisy or internally inconsistent (e.g. electricity-origin stacked bars summing oddly, some sector evolution numbers), suggesting OCR/transcription issues but also that not every chart was airtight

The GA/compact optimization is demonstrated only for Texas as a worked example and a single γ=0.667 borrowed from unrelated trade literature; goals for other states lean on the same coefficient without state-specific calibration
Policy elasticities (e.g. +1% budget -> +9.9% A3) are asserted as outputs without a clearly shown derivation/sensitivity sweep, so they read more like illustrative figures than validated results

California is assumed to 'benefit little' and largely excluded from the compact optimization (self-sufficiency ≈1), which the team concedes may be wrong — it could still gain from cheaper imports/exports
reusable_patterns: The 'closed-loop index spine': define a small set of named, equation-backed indices early (here A1-A4 under a 3E umbrella) and reuse the SAME indices for ranking, forecasting, optimization, and policy — a future team should pick 3-5 indices and make every section refer back to them

Hybrid MCDM template: Entropy weights (objective) + TOPSIS (distance to ideal) + PROMETHEE (pairwise outranking), blended S = ζΦ+ + τD-. Reusable verbatim for any 'rank these N options' sub-question, with the justification that it captures both absolute and relative comparison
VAR-with-AIC-and-ADF forecasting recipe for multivariate time-series prediction sub-questions, plus a back-test accuracy table to defend the forecast — directly reusable code is in the R appendix
Constrained optimization + Genetic Algorithm pattern for any allocation/'design a policy' sub-question, including an embedded behavioral penalty term (η/E^i for trust) to mathematize a soft factor

Translate optimization outputs into goals via elasticity/coefficient relations (ΔGDP = γ·p·ΔE), then map each recommended ACTION to a specific index it moves — gives clean, defensible 'do X to raise metric Y by Z%' advice

ICM packaging moves: front-load a decision-maker memo in plain language, add a dedicated data-cleaning section for 'data'-type problems, and always include an honest Strengths/Weaknesses plus runnable appendix code
Acronym-naming discipline (ECPM, SDPM, 3E, EM-TOPSIS-PROMETHEE) to give graders memorable handles for each model
